articles.title: Causal mediation analysis with one or multiple mediators: A comparative study
articles.blurb: This is the paper to send to anyone still defaulting to classical mediation analysis in observational data. Abécassis et al. comprehensively benchmark multiply-robust and double machine learning estimators against parametric approaches, and DML wins across nearly every simulation scenario — especially in the nonlinear confounding settings that define real-world EHR data. The UK Biobank application showing brain morphology mediates a substantial portion of the hypertension-cognition effect makes the methodology concrete.

articles.title: Causal estimands and target trials for the effect of lag time to treatment of cancer patients
articles.blurb: The Waiting Time Paradox — where sicker patients see doctors sooner and appear to have worse outcomes from faster treatment — is one of the most treacherous confounding traps in oncology RWE. Goncalves & Franco formalize the causal estimand problem for treatment lag time and propose a full target trial protocol to address it. This is the missing methods paper for anyone analyzing time-to-treatment data in cancer using EHR or administrative claims.

articles.title: STRASS 2 target trial emulation: Bridging the gap between trial efficacy and real-world effectiveness
articles.blurb: Retroperitoneal sarcoma is rare and heterogeneous — making target trial emulation with the national cancer database especially valuable here. Talathi et al. find no survival benefit from neoadjuvant chemo vs. upfront surgery, and the finding holds across multivariable Cox, IPTW, and TTE analyses. The three-estimator consistency is exactly the triangulation practitioners should demand before treating observational findings as definitive.

articles.title: irAE-GPT: leveraging large language models to identify immune-related adverse events in electronic health records and clinical trial datasets
articles.blurb: Pharmacovigilance is one of the most credible RWE use cases for LLMs, and irAE-GPT delivers a large-scale, multi-institution validation across both EHR and clinical trial text. GPT-4o achieves the best performance, but the critical finding is the causal attribution failure: models correctly identify adverse events but struggle to establish that they were caused by the immunotherapy — the exact limitation that matters for regulatory-grade safety signal detection.

articles.title: Assessing the quality of AI-generated and physician-written discharge summaries: evaluation of an EHR-integrated tool in a Dutch academic hospital
articles.blurb: Most LLM-in-the-EHR papers test drafts in simulated settings; this one evaluates 292 fully automated summaries from a production-deployed system across multiple specialties at a real academic hospital. The headline finding — identical total quality scores — is striking, but the trust gap (85% vs. 95% full clinician trust) is the operationally meaningful number that should govern deployment decisions.

articles.title: A Target Trial Emulation Study of SGLT2 Inhibitors, GLP-1 Receptor Agonists, and Combination Therapy in Preventing Kidney Failure in Type 2 Diabetes
articles.blurb: This paper delivers a clean, clinically actionable comparison using target trial emulation on a large real-world claims database. The finding that SGLT2 inhibitors outperform GLP-1 agonists for kidney failure prevention — and that combination therapy may further benefit high-risk patients with CKD or heart failure — is precisely the kind of RWE practitioners need to guide prescribing decisions. The new-user, active-comparator design is the gold standard here.

articles.title: An AI Agent for Automated Causal Inference in Epidemiology
articles.blurb: EpiCausalX is the first end-to-end LLM agent that automates the full causal inference pipeline in epidemiology — from multi-database literature retrieval to confounder identification to interactive DAG visualization. This is what AI-augmented causal epidemiology actually looks like in practice, not just a chatbot wrapper around a textbook. The architecture signals where agentic causal workflows are heading in 2026.

articles.title: Evidence triangulator: using large language models to extract and synthesize causal evidence across study designs
articles.blurb: Shi et al. tackle a genuinely hard problem — automating evidence triangulation across heterogeneous study designs — and show LLMs can extract directional effect and statistical significance information with high accuracy. The two-step approach (exposure-outcome concepts first, then relation extraction) is the key methodological insight worth borrowing. Practically, this is a game-changer for regulatory and HTA evidence synthesis.

articles.title: Double Machine Learning for Causal Inference in High-Dimensional Electronic Health Records
articles.blurb: For pharmacoepidemiologists still defaulting to IPTW in high-dimensional EHR settings, this is a wake-up call. Du et al. show that offset-DML consistently outperforms IPTW on both bias and empirical standard error — especially in sparse-data scenarios common in clinical practice. The plasmode simulation grounded in real clinical data gives these results far more credibility than a purely synthetic benchmark.

articles.title: Can Contemporary Large Language Models Provide the Domain Knowledge Needed for Causal Inference? Evaluating Automated Causal Graph Discovery Through an ASCVD Case Study
articles.blurb: Aziz and Brookhart benchmark GPT-4o and GPT-o1 for automated DAG generation in cardiovascular pharmacoepidemiology — and find current LLMs can assist but cannot replace human domain expertise. This is required reading before you hand your confounder selection to a language model. The ASCVD case study exposes systematic failure modes: LLMs miss temporality, conflate proxies with mediators, and hallucinate causal paths.
